- ○ **Fixture scope/lifetime — trial-scoped (fresh per trial) vs experiment-scoped (shared across trials) vs ad-hoc? How is scope declared and enforced, and does it interact with the dep DAG (e.g., a trial-scoped fixture depending on an experiment-scoped one)?** — MVP0 ships trial-scope only — every fixture is fresh per trial, torn down at trial end. Reserve hooks for v2 without implementing: 'scope' field on fixture declarations (only 'trial' valid in v1), 'mutability' field ('mutable | read-only | snapshotted', informational in v1), DAG validator stub aware of 'lab > experiment > trial' ordering. → [`2026-05-01-fixture-scopelifetime-trial-scoped-fresh-per-trial-c-mom19282.md`](../records/2026-05-01-fixture-scopelifetime-trial-scoped-fresh-per-trial-c-mom19282.md)
- ○ **How do we declaratively express the scenario — the workload that runs inside a trial after setup (commissions to post, ordering, end-conditions like queue-drains/timeout/rig-completes)? The spec doc currently assumes a human posts commissions manually; a real toolkit needs a scenario primitive.** — Scenario IS a rig template, dispatched via existing Spider/Reckoner machinery. No separate scenario primitive. → [`2026-05-01-how-do-we-declaratively-express-the-scenario-the-w-c-mom193ka.md`](../records/2026-05-01-how-do-we-declaratively-express-the-scenario-the-w-c-mom193ka.md)
- ○ **How is trial config carried into the rig — writ body, ext, or both?** — ext.laboratory.config carries the structured trial config (fixtures, plugin pins, files, brief refs, probe selections). Body stays free-form prose for human description (variant intent, hypothesis, notes). → [`2026-05-01-how-is-trial-config-carried-into-the-rig-writ-body-c-mom9vkls.md`](../records/2026-05-01-how-is-trial-config-carried-into-the-rig-writ-body-c-mom9vkls.md)
- ◇ *(parked)* Cross-guild engine surface — when does shell-out via 'nsg --guild-root <other>' stop being good enough? MVP path is CLI shell-out; track for symptoms (auth, perf, observability across the boundary) that motivate a real cross-guild engine surface.
- ● **Implementation — turn the Laboratory MVP0 design into a working apparatus. Children decompose the build into commissionable units; each child becomes a brief + posted commission when ready to dispatch. First few items (framework verification, plugin reshape, schema/CLI) unblock everything else; engines and archive can parallelize once the skeleton lands.**
  - ○ **Verify framework prerequisites — confirm 'nsg init', 'nsg plugin install' (does it accept git SHAs / refs, not just npm semver?), and 'nsg codex add' invocation surfaces against current framework source. Confirm gh org permissions for codex repo create/delete. Surface gaps to negotiate with framework before scripts land.** — All load-bearing prerequisites confirmed against live framework source: non-anima engines first-class via { status: 'completed' } EngineRunResult (precedent: spider.seal does git ops sync and returns yields); 'nsg plugin install' accepts SHAs natively via npm git+URL#sha syntax; ClerkApi.registerWritType is the writ-type contribution path (called from apparatus.start); SpiderKit.rigTemplates + rigTemplateMappings contribute templates+routings; Fabricator scans kit.engines for designs. One spec correction: 'nsg codex add' takes only (name, remoteUrl) — no --sha flag; pinning happens earlier by pushing fresh GH repo at the desired SHA before codex-add. → [`2026-05-01-verify-framework-prerequisites-confirm-nsg-init-ns-c-moma9psb.md`](../records/2026-05-01-verify-framework-prerequisites-confirm-nsg-init-ns-c-moma9psb.md)
  - ○ **Reshape the laboratory plugin from no-op stub to apparatus skeleton — package structure, kit-contribution exports, plugin metadata, TS types module. Foundation that subsequent children build on.** — Apparatus skeleton landed in /workspace/nexus-mk2/packages/laboratory: requires=[stacks,clerk], recommends=[spider,fabricator,codexes], supportKit empty (engines+templates+tools land in subsequent children), start() registers TRIAL_WRIT_TYPE_CONFIG via clerk.registerWritType. types.ts exports TRIAL_TYPE_NAME, TRIAL_WRIT_TYPE_CONFIG (mirrors mandate state machine: new → open → completed/failed/cancelled with stuck off open; childrenBehavior populated for v2 experiment-parent compatibility), and the LaboratoryTrialConfig schema (fixtures, scenario, probes, archive — provisional shapes; refined as engine work progresses). Legacy CDC data-mirroring code purged: dispatch-removal.test.ts and loom-composition.test.ts (orphan verification scripts for completed framework commissions) deleted; old LaboratoryConfig replaced with empty placeholder. → [`2026-05-01-reshape-the-laboratory-plugin-from-no-op-stub-to-a-c-moma9qyz.md`](../records/2026-05-01-reshape-the-laboratory-plugin-from-no-op-stub-to-a-c-moma9qyz.md)
  - ○ **Define the trial writ type and 'ext.laboratory.config' schema — TS types + JSON Schema, registered as a writ type in the lab's kit contribution. Schema mirrors the existing run.yaml shape from experiments/infrastructure/setup-and-artifacts.md.** — Trial writ type and ext.laboratory.config schema landed in packages/laboratory/src/types.ts. TRIAL_WRIT_TYPE_CONFIG mirrors mandate's state machine (new → open → completed/failed/cancelled with stuck off open) plus children-cascade triggers populated for v2 experiment-parent compatibility. → [`2026-05-01-define-the-trial-writ-type-and-extlaboratoryconfig-c-moma9sg5.md`](../records/2026-05-01-define-the-trial-writ-type-and-extlaboratoryconfig-c-moma9sg5.md)
  - ○ **Author manifest YAML format and the 'nsg lab trial post --manifest <file>' CLI — manifest schema (same shape as ext.laboratory.config plus optional 'description' field), parser, validator, writ-post via existing CLI surfaces.** — Manifest CLI landed: src/manifest.ts (zod schema mirroring LaboratoryTrialConfig with optional title, description, parentId, codex), parser (YAML → schema validation → fixture DAG cross-validation via topoSortFixtures → probe id uniqueness), and manifestToWritShape (splits into clerk.post inputs + ext.laboratory.config payload). src/tools/trial-post.ts wraps the parser and chains: clerk.post(type='trial') → clerk.setWritExt('laboratory', {config}) → clerk.transition('new'→'open') unless --draft. Codex auto-resolution mirrors commission-post (inherit from parentId; default to single registered codex; fail when multiple/none). 26 manifest tests added (55 total): YAML parse errors, schema rejections (slug shape, missing required, malformed ids, mutability enum), defaults (empty fixtures/probes, empty givens), cross-field DAG validation passthrough, probe id uniqueness, manifestToWritShape (title default 'Trial: <slug>', description→body, parentId/codex pass-through, trialConfig packing). → [`2026-05-01-author-manifest-yaml-format-and-the-nsg-lab-trial-c-moma9ty6.md`](../records/2026-05-01-author-manifest-yaml-format-and-the-nsg-lab-trial-c-moma9ty6.md)
  - ○ **Author the post-and-collect-default rig template — composes fixture-setup, scenario, probe, teardown, and archive engines from the writ's ext.laboratory.config. Topological sort of fixture deps happens at template instantiation; rig itself stays a flat sequence.** — post-and-collect-default rig template landed with the dynamic-graft pattern (same shape as spider.implement-loop): the static template declares one head engine ('lab.orchestrate') reading the writ; orchestrate at runtime reads ext.laboratory.config, topo-sorts fixtures, and emits the per-trial graft chain (fixture-setup → scenario → probes → archive → fixture-teardown-in-reverse) with sequential teardowns to keep teardown failures from shredding sibling teardowns. SupportKit wiring complete: rigTemplates['post-and-collect-default'] + rigTemplateMappings.trial. → [`2026-05-01-author-the-post-and-collect-default-rig-template-c-c-moma9vrm.md`](../records/2026-05-01-author-the-post-and-collect-default-rig-template-c-c-moma9vrm.md)
  - ○ **Implement codex fixture engines (setup + teardown) — clone upstream at base SHA, push to GH as private repo with conventional name 'experiment-<X-num>-<slug>-run-<NNN>-<variant>'; teardown does 'gh repo delete'. Fail-loud safety check on teardown if the repo has unmerged content not in the archive.** — Real implementations of lab.codex-setup + lab.codex-teardown landed (engines/codex-fixture.ts). Local-bare-repo flow per Sean: setup clones upstream → checkout baseSha → init bare at <labHost>/.nexus/laboratory/codexes/<name>.git → push → register via Scriptorium.add. → [`2026-05-01-implement-codex-fixture-engines-setup-teardown-clo-c-moma9y1k.md`](../records/2026-05-01-implement-codex-fixture-engines-setup-teardown-clo-c-moma9y1k.md)
  - ○ **Implement guild fixture engines (setup + teardown) — 'nsg init', 'nsg plugin install' per pin, 'nsg codex add', deep-merge guild.config, copy files; teardown rm -rfs the guild dir. Refuses to overwrite an existing guild dir on setup; refuses to teardown if archive hasn't run.** — Real implementations of lab.guild-setup + lab.guild-teardown landed (engines/guild-fixture.ts). Setup: nsg init <path> → per-pin nsg plugin install → codex registration via duck-type discovery (any upstream yield with codexName+remoteUrl gets registered) → deep-merge guild.json → file copies (absolute sourcePath only in v1; manifest-relative resolution is future polish). → [`2026-05-01-implement-guild-fixture-engines-setup-teardown-nsg-c-momaa03d.md`](../records/2026-05-01-implement-guild-fixture-engines-setup-teardown-nsg-c-momaa03d.md)
  - ○ **Implement cross-guild scenario engines — 'commission-post-xguild' (shells out to 'nsg commission-post --guild-root <X>') and 'wait-for-writ-terminal-xguild' (polls writ status across guilds via --guild-root). Sequential commission posting only in v1; concurrent posting deferred.** — Cross-guild scenario engines landed (engines/scenario-xguild.ts). lab.commission-post-xguild posts a commission via 'nsg --guild-root <test-guild> commission-post', then INLINE polls until terminal (default waitForTerminal=true) — single engine fits the rig template's single-scenario-engine slot for the canonical 'post and wait' flow. Optional opt-out via waitForTerminal=false yields just {writId, postedAt}. → [`2026-05-01-implement-cross-guild-scenario-engines-commission-c-momaa1vt.md`](../records/2026-05-01-implement-cross-guild-scenario-engines-commission-c-momaa1vt.md)
  - ○ **Implement the three standard probe engines — lab.probe-stacks-dump (one row per test-guild source-row to lab-trial-stacks-dumps; pure JSON1 indexing), lab.probe-git-range (one row per commit to lab-trial-codex-commits; 10MB per-diff cap as fail-loud tripwire), lab.probe-trial-context (rig id + template name + framework SHA + resolved plugin pins + manifest snapshot as summary-only data; no bulk book). Each probe owns its book schema, run() handler, and extract() handler under src/probes/<name>/, and registers with the probe registry (c-momkil4p). Spec: packages/laboratory/README.md.** — Three standard probes implemented at packages/laboratory/src/probes/{trial-context,stacks-dump,git-range}.ts. lab.probe-trial-context: summary-only (no book), captures trialId/rigId/rigTemplate/frameworkVersion/pluginsInstalled/manifestSnapshot/capturedAt. lab.probe-stacks-dump: opens the test guild's nexus.db read-only via better-sqlite3 (already a transitive dep through Stacks), enumerates books_* tables from sqlite_master, writes one row per source-row to lab-trial-stacks-dumps with sourceBook resolution via prefix-match against the test guild's installed plugin list (preserves '<owner>/<book>' form when resolvable, raw books_… table name otherwise); summary {bookCounts: {...}, totalRows, capturedAt}. lab.probe-git-range: walks  in the codex bare repo, captures one row per commit (with --shortstat parsing for filesChanged/insertions/deletions), fails loud on any single diff > 10MB tripwire, summary {codexName, baseSha, headSha, commitCount, totalDiffBytes}. Each probe has a colocated extract() method that materializes its captured data: trial-context.yaml at top level; stacks-export/<safe-stem>.json per source book; codex-history/{commits-manifest.yaml, NNNN-<sha-12>.patch} for git range. → [`2026-05-01-implement-the-three-standard-probe-engines-labprob-c-momaa3w7.md`](../records/2026-05-01-implement-the-three-standard-probe-engines-labprob-c-momaa3w7.md)
  - ○ **Design and implement the archive engine — open sub-question on output target (lab guild books vs sanctum mirror vs hybrid?), per-trial storage layout, linkage from trial writ to archive artifacts. Design conclusion lands in this click; implementation follows in same scope.** — DB-authoritative archive with on-demand filesystem materialization. Archive engine owns one minimal index book — lab-trial-archives — with row shape { id, trialId (FK to clerk/writs), status, archivedAt, probes[{id, engineId, summary}] }; archive engine has no schema opinions about probe data. → [`2026-05-01-design-and-implement-the-archive-engine-open-sub-q-c-momaa5o9.md`](../records/2026-05-01-design-and-implement-the-archive-engine-open-sub-q-c-momaa5o9.md)
  - ○ **Smoke test — run a synthetic trial end-to-end through the apparatus (trivial brief, minimal plugin set) to validate the pipeline before porting real experiments. Lives in the laboratory plugin's tests.** — Codified pipeline smoke test landed at packages/laboratory/src/integration.test.ts with 7 test cases. Strategy chosen: synthetic-engine pipeline test, NOT full Spider/Animator stack. → [`2026-05-01-smoke-test-run-a-synthetic-trial-end-to-end-throug-c-momaa75l.md`](../records/2026-05-01-smoke-test-run-a-synthetic-trial-end-to-end-throug-c-momaa75l.md)
  - ○ **Retire and migrate experiments/infrastructure/setup-and-artifacts.md — the apparatus replaces the spec's standalone bash scripts. Decide: delete the spec doc, archive it as historical, or rewrite it as a usage guide pointing at the apparatus. Conclude c-molveg89 in the same step.** — Decision: archive-as-historical, not delete. Moved experiments/infrastructure/setup-and-artifacts.md to docs/archive/deprecated-docs/experimental-infrastructure-setup-and-artifacts.md with a top-of-file header pointing forward to the apparatus and packages/laboratory/README.md. → [`2026-05-01-retire-and-migrate-experimentsinfrastructuresetup-c-momaa8mk.md`](../records/2026-05-01-retire-and-migrate-experimentsinfrastructuresetup-c-momaa8mk.md)
  - ○ **Documentation — manifest authoring guide, laboratory plugin overview for /workspace/nexus/docs/architecture/, end-user how-to for evaluating prompts/plugins via trials. Audiences: nexus dev (architecture), end users (plugin docs).** — Documentation pass complete. Fleshed out packages/laboratory/README.md's Authoring trials section with: a complete minimal manifest example (all four engine pairs + three probes wired), manifest-shape rules (slug/id/DAG/teardown override/givens), the post-trial CLI workflow (writ list / show, rig list, lab trial-show / extract / export-book), and authoring tips (codex baseSha picks the snapshot, plugin-pin syntax with the file: vs link: pitfall, default fixture isolation, probe parallelism, absolute briefPath in v1). → [`2026-05-01-documentation-manifest-authoring-guide-laboratory-c-momaaa3t.md`](../records/2026-05-01-documentation-manifest-authoring-guide-laboratory-c-momaaa3t.md)
  - ☐ *(task → todo.md)* Port the first real-world trial — pick whichever P3 experiment is next (likely X016 orientation suppression), author its manifest, run it through the apparatus end-to-end. Validates the apparatus on real research work and produces the first real findings via the new pipeline.
  - ○ **Probe registry + extraction-dispatch contract — probe engines self-declare an extract() handler; nsg lab trial-extract dispatches by engineId via the registry rather than a hardcoded mapping. MVP-required: gates the trial-extract tool. Includes the registry pattern, the contract shape (extract(trialId, targetDir, guild) → file list), and built-in registrations for lab.probe-stacks-dump, lab.probe-git-range, and lab.probe-trial-context.** — Implemented as a structural type-guard pattern over the Fabricator's existing engine registry — no separate probe registry. ProbeEngineDesign extends EngineDesign with extract(args:{trialId, targetDir}) → {files:[{path, bytes}]}; isProbeEngineDesign type guard checks for the function. → [`2026-05-01-probe-registry-extraction-dispatch-contract-probe-c-momkil4p.md`](../records/2026-05-01-probe-registry-extraction-dispatch-contract-probe-c-momkil4p.md)
  - ○ **Implement lab.archive engine — read probe yields from upstream context, write one row to lab-trial-archives with id+trialId+archivedAt+probes[{id, engineId, summary}]. Atomic per-row via SQLite. Replace the current archive-presence safety check on teardown engines from 'context.upstream.archive defined' to 'archive row exists for this trialId'. Conclude this click on implementation; design conclusion lives at sibling c-momaa5o9 and full spec at packages/laboratory/README.md.** — Implemented at packages/laboratory/src/archive/engine.ts. Reads probe summaries from context.upstream (PROBE_ID(probe.id) keys), pairs each probe declaration with its yield via buildProbeEntries (preserves declaration order regardless of crawl order, throws on missing upstream — orchestration bug surface, wraps non-object yields in {value:...} so summary is always an object), writes one atomic row to lab-trial-archives with {id (generated), trialId (FK), archivedAt (ISO), probes:[{id, engineId, summary}]}. → [`2026-05-01-implement-labarchive-engine-read-probe-yields-from-c-momkqtn5.md`](../records/2026-05-01-implement-labarchive-engine-read-probe-yields-from-c-momkqtn5.md)
  - ● Failed-trial orphan cleanup — when guild-setup fails after codex-setup succeeds, the rig fails without running teardowns, leaving codexes registered in the lab-host's Scriptorium and bare repos on disk. Two design directions: (1) setup engines grab a release-on-rig-terminal handle that fires on any rig terminal-state, OR (2) teardowns run unconditionally on rig failure (currently they only run when the upstream chain succeeded). Surfaced during live verifies. Cosmetic for now (orphans don't run code) but failed-trial dev loops accumulate cruft.
  - ● Daemon vs CLI Scriptorium state drift — "nsg codex remove" from the CLI runs in a separate process and updates guild.json, but the running daemon in-memory Scriptorium state is not notified. The daemon next guild.json write reverts the orphan removals. Vibers currently has three cosmetic orphan codexes (verify-fixtures-{1267530e,2244ce82,40dd2663}) that will only clear on daemon restart. Cosmetic (orphan bare repos do not run code) but means CLI-side cleanup does not stick. Possible fixes: (1) CLI-side codex-remove signals the daemon via Clockworks event to reload Scriptorium, OR (2) Scriptorium adopts a watch-and-reconcile pattern on guild.json changes, OR (3) document the daemon-restart requirement and fail-loud when CLI-side codex changes are made with a daemon running.
- ◇ *(parked)* v2: introduce 'experiment' as a grouping concept above trial writs — when comparative views (cross-trial dashboards, integrating verdict, multi-variant analysis) earn their keep. Likely a new writ type that owns child trial writs, or a separate 'studies' book. Hold until comparative-analysis features have a motivating need.

1	# Design the Reckoner scheduler kit-contribution shape — factory signature (what does the factory receive: clerk + stacks + scheduler-specific config? a curated sub-API surface?), kit-static seal lifecycle (phase:started, per-plugin-load-cycle), how guild.json's reckoner.scheduler selector resolves against contributed scheduler ids, and how schedulerConfig is typed (per-id schema validation? opaque pass-through?).
2	
3	- **id**: `c-moisx6fx-9abfa7024c20`
4	- **status**: concluded
5	- **resolved**: 2026-04-28T17:19:40.318Z
6	- **context**: under [Design the Reckoner — petition scheduler that accepts petitions from registered ](../threads/unlock-autonomous-hopper-based-operation-for-nexus-c-mo1mq8ry.md)
7	
8	## Decision
9	
10	**Pattern:** Direct-instance kit contribution mirroring Fabricator's EngineDesign — no factory wrapper. Schedulers reach for state/services via guild() at evaluate time (matches the EngineDesign precedent at fabricator.ts:108: "the engine looks up whatever it needs via guild()"). No DI from Reckoner core.
11	
12	**Scheduler interface:**
13	
14	```ts
15	interface Scheduler<TConfig = unknown> {
16	  id:           string;       // {pluginId}.{kebab-suffix}, mirrors petitioner source-id grammar
17	  description:  string;       // operator-facing one-liner
18	  evaluate(input: SchedulerInput<TConfig>): Promise<readonly SchedulerDecision[]>;
19	  validateConfig?(raw: unknown): TConfig;   // optional; called per tick to validate guild.json schedulerConfig
20	}
21	
22	interface SchedulerInput<TConfig> {
23	  candidates: readonly HeldWrit[];   // post source-validation, post disabledSources filter
24	  capacity:   CapacitySnapshot;
25	  now:        Date;
26	  config:     TConfig;                // post-validation; opaque to core, typed by scheduler
27	}
28	
29	interface SchedulerDecision {
30	  writId:  string;
31	  outcome: 'approve' | 'defer' | 'decline';
32	  reason:  string;
33	  weight?: number;                    // optional; logged into Reckonings
34	}
35	```
36	
37	`TConfig` is a TypeScript ergonomic — core stores schedulers as `Scheduler<unknown>` and trusts each scheduler's `validateConfig` to produce the shape its `evaluate` expects.
38	
39	**Lifecycle / sealing:** Mirrors petitioners exactly. Plugins contribute via `kit.schedulers` array; id grammar `{pluginId}.{kebab}` validated; duplicate-id hard-fail naming both contributing kits; registry sealed at `phase:started`; per-plugin-load-cycle framing applies. Default schedulers (`reckoner.always-approve`, `reckoner.priority-walk`) are contributed by the reckoner package's own kit — treated identically to user-contributed schedulers.
40	
41	**Selector resolution (`guild.json` `reckoner.scheduler`):**
42	1. At `phase:started`, after registry seal, Reckoner reads `reckoner.scheduler`.
43	2. Unset/absent → default to `reckoner.always-approve`. Logged at info level.
44	3. Set but unregistered → fail-loud at startup with the list of registered ids.
45	4. Resolved scheduler reference cached for the duration of the seal.
46	
47	**Per-tick config flow:**
48	1. Tick fires. Core re-reads `reckoner.schedulerConfig` from guild.json (matches the resolveConfig D20 re-read pattern at reckoner.ts:364).
49	2. If the scheduler defines `validateConfig`, core calls it. Throw → log fail-loud + skip the tick.
50	3. Validated config goes into `SchedulerInput.config`; `evaluate` is called.
51	
52	**schedulerConfig typing:** Opaque pass-through (`unknown`) at the core surface. Validation lives entirely in each scheduler's `validateConfig`. No central schema registry — schedulers choose their own validation library (zod, hand-rolled, etc.). `reckoner.always-approve` doesn't define `validateConfig`; any value is accepted-and-ignored.
53	
54	**Failure modes (all fail-loud where shown):**
55	
56	| Failure | Behavior |
57	|---|---|
58	| Duplicate scheduler id across kits | hard error at startup, names both kits |
59	| `reckoner.scheduler` references unregistered id | hard error at startup, lists registered ids |
60	| `validateConfig` throws | log fail-loud, skip the tick (apparatus stays up) |
61	| `evaluate` throws | log fail-loud, skip the tick (no decisions applied, no Reckonings rows written) |
62	| `evaluate` returns decisions for non-candidate writ ids | log + ignore those decisions |
63	| `evaluate` returns multiple decisions for one writ id | log fail-loud, skip the tick |
64	
65	**Out of scope (deferred):**
66	- **Tick-timer registration mechanism** — Clockworks doesn't support imperative schedule registration or kit-contributed standing orders today. Adopting Option B (extend Clockworks for kit-contributed standing orders, then Reckoner contributes both relay + standing order) — opened as child click.
67	- Multi-scheduler dispatch (one scheduler per Reckoner instance in v0; layered routing future).
68	- Lifecycle hooks beyond `evaluate` + `validateConfig` (no `onTickStart` / `onApprove` / etc.).
69	- Async scheduler initialization (direct-instance pattern has no init step; lazy-init on first evaluate is allowed).
70	- Hot-reloading schedulers without restart (kit-static seal means swap requires apparatus restart).
71

# Design the Reckoner scheduler kit-contribution shape — factory signature (what does the factory receive: clerk + stacks + scheduler-specific config? a curated sub-API surface?), kit-static seal lifecycle (phase:started, per-plugin-load-cycle), how guild.json's reckoner.scheduler selector resolves against contributed scheduler ids, and how schedulerConfig is typed (per-id schema validation? opaque pass-through?).

- **id**: `c-moisx6fx-9abfa7024c20`
- **status**: concluded
- **resolved**: 2026-04-28T17:19:40.318Z
- **context**: under [Design the Reckoner — petition scheduler that accepts petitions from registered ](../threads/unlock-autonomous-hopper-based-operation-for-nexus-c-mo1mq8ry.md)

## Decision

**Pattern:** Direct-instance kit contribution mirroring Fabricator's EngineDesign — no factory wrapper. Schedulers reach for state/services via guild() at evaluate time (matches the EngineDesign precedent at fabricator.ts:108: "the engine looks up whatever it needs via guild()"). No DI from Reckoner core.

**Scheduler interface:**

```ts
interface Scheduler<TConfig = unknown> {
  id:           string;       // {pluginId}.{kebab-suffix}, mirrors petitioner source-id grammar
  description:  string;       // operator-facing one-liner
  evaluate(input: SchedulerInput<TConfig>): Promise<readonly SchedulerDecision[]>;
  validateConfig?(raw: unknown): TConfig;   // optional; called per tick to validate guild.json schedulerConfig
}

interface SchedulerInput<TConfig> {
  candidates: readonly HeldWrit[];   // post source-validation, post disabledSources filter
  capacity:   CapacitySnapshot;
  now:        Date;
  config:     TConfig;                // post-validation; opaque to core, typed by scheduler
}

interface SchedulerDecision {
  writId:  string;
  outcome: 'approve' | 'defer' | 'decline';
  reason:  string;
  weight?: number;                    // optional; logged into Reckonings
}
```

`TConfig` is a TypeScript ergonomic — core stores schedulers as `Scheduler<unknown>` and trusts each scheduler's `validateConfig` to produce the shape its `evaluate` expects.

**Lifecycle / sealing:** Mirrors petitioners exactly. Plugins contribute via `kit.schedulers` array; id grammar `{pluginId}.{kebab}` validated; duplicate-id hard-fail naming both contributing kits; registry sealed at `phase:started`; per-plugin-load-cycle framing applies. Default schedulers (`reckoner.always-approve`, `reckoner.priority-walk`) are contributed by the reckoner package's own kit — treated identically to user-contributed schedulers.

**Selector resolution (`guild.json` `reckoner.scheduler`):**
1. At `phase:started`, after registry seal, Reckoner reads `reckoner.scheduler`.
2. Unset/absent → default to `reckoner.always-approve`. Logged at info level.
3. Set but unregistered → fail-loud at startup with the list of registered ids.
4. Resolved scheduler reference cached for the duration of the seal.

**Per-tick config flow:**
1. Tick fires. Core re-reads `reckoner.schedulerConfig` from guild.json (matches the resolveConfig D20 re-read pattern at reckoner.ts:364).
2. If the scheduler defines `validateConfig`, core calls it. Throw → log fail-loud + skip the tick.
3. Validated config goes into `SchedulerInput.config`; `evaluate` is called.

**schedulerConfig typing:** Opaque pass-through (`unknown`) at the core surface. Validation lives entirely in each scheduler's `validateConfig`. No central schema registry — schedulers choose their own validation library (zod, hand-rolled, etc.). `reckoner.always-approve` doesn't define `validateConfig`; any value is accepted-and-ignored.

**Failure modes (all fail-loud where shown):**

| Failure | Behavior |
|---|---|
| Duplicate scheduler id across kits | hard error at startup, names both kits |
| `reckoner.scheduler` references unregistered id | hard error at startup, lists registered ids |
| `validateConfig` throws | log fail-loud, skip the tick (apparatus stays up) |
| `evaluate` throws | log fail-loud, skip the tick (no decisions applied, no Reckonings rows written) |
| `evaluate` returns decisions for non-candidate writ ids | log + ignore those decisions |
| `evaluate` returns multiple decisions for one writ id | log fail-loud, skip the tick |

**Out of scope (deferred):**
- **Tick-timer registration mechanism** — Clockworks doesn't support imperative schedule registration or kit-contributed standing orders today. Adopting Option B (extend Clockworks for kit-contributed standing orders, then Reckoner contributes both relay + standing order) — opened as child click.
- Multi-scheduler dispatch (one scheduler per Reckoner instance in v0; layered routing future).
- Lifecycle hooks beyond `evaluate` + `validateConfig` (no `onTickStart` / `onApprove` / etc.).
- Async scheduler initialization (direct-instance pattern has no init step; lazy-init on first evaluate is allowed).
- Hot-reloading schedulers without restart (kit-static seal means swap requires apparatus restart).

content.content:
1	# Unlock autonomous hopper-based operation for Nexus
2	
3	- **root id**: `c-mo1mq8ry-c8cf4810db6c`
4	- **status**: live
5	- **created**: 2026-04-16
6	
7	---
8	
9	- ○ **Redesign Astrolabe planner for intent-and-constraints specs with tiered decision model** — Design closed. The planner redesign resolved into: (1) intent-and-constraints specs (not prescriptive implementation) — concluded children c-mo1uw0ah, c-mo1uw15a; (2) binary pick+record / surface model replacing the originally-proposed 4 tiers — concluded child c-mo1mq9qa (tier model scrapped), c-mo1mq9f6 (preference model dropped as not-MVP); (3) a five-criterion razor for which decisions surface — c-mo1yb8aj; (4) three codified policy defaults that account for ~40% of observed overrides — c-mo1yb8nf; (5) empirical baseline of ~3.7% override rate across 38 specs for future comparison — c-mo1yb8zb; (6) ratification-not-approval as the patron-interaction model — c-mo1uw2ml. → [`2026-04-16-redesign-astrolabe-planner-for-intent-and-constrai-c-mo1mq93f.md`](../records/2026-04-16-redesign-astrolabe-planner-for-intent-and-constrai-c-mo1mq93f.md)
10	- ○ **Choose recovery policy for seal-engine rebase conflicts** — Concluded. Manual-merge recovery tail shipped as commission w-mo2280qu (nexus commit 19921b7) — on Scriptorium seal failure with a rebase-conflict signature, the seal engine grafts a two-engine tail: (1) a manual-merge quick engine summoning the new spider.mender anima to reconcile conflicts in the draft worktree, (2) a retry seal engine attempting fast-forward push. → [`2026-04-22-choose-recovery-policy-for-seal-engine-rebase-conf-c-mo1mqb0r.md`](../records/2026-04-22-choose-recovery-policy-for-seal-engine-rebase-conf-c-mo1mqb0r.md)
11	- ● **Design concurrency-control substrate for parallel planning rigs**
12	  - ● Design OCC validation of plans against observed read sets
13	  - ☐ *(task → todo.md)* Resolve plan-vs-plan conflicts when two Astrolabe sessions overlap
14	- ○ **Identify what actually predicts patron-override probability, to sharpen the razor beyond shape-matching** — Patron-override rate is not predicted by any single analysis axis (stakes/category/observable/confidence are all weak discriminators — base rate 6.9%, none above 10%). It is predicted by two orthogonal tests: (1) Reach — the choice must radiate beyond the unit being built; pattern-extending decisions do not need patron review, (2) Patch cost — if the outcome is cheap to reverse via follow-up commission, auto-decide. → [`2026-04-17-identify-what-actually-predicts-patron-override-pr-c-mo2c836f.md`](../records/2026-04-17-identify-what-actually-predicts-patron-override-pr-c-mo2c836f.md)
15	- ● **Stage 1: Run posted commissions unattended — hopper execution and operational guardrails (cost bounds, intervention notifications)**
16	  - ● Design cost tracking and token budget allocation (Coinmaster/Purse/Tithe)
17	  - ○ **Implement notifications when the system requires human intervention** — Design fully resolved. Subclicks: triggers (c-mob1vyb2), channel (c-mob1vyyi), response surface (c-mob1w04q), rate-limit (c-mob1w0ql), authoritative source (c-mob1w13b), revised pulse shape (c-mobzl35x supersedes c-mob1vzjy). → [`2026-04-23-implement-notifications-when-the-system-requires-h-c-mo1z3teo.md`](../records/2026-04-23-implement-notifications-when-the-system-requires-h-c-mo1z3teo.md)
18	  - ● **Deliver autonomous-but-serial hopper operation (maxConcurrentEngines: 1) as the MVP autonomy milestone**
19	    - ○ **Design intake tier model (backlog / ready pool / parked) to decouple dump from dispatch** — Dissolved. The new/open writ status split already provides the captured-vs-committed decoupling this click was trying to build: writs in 'new' are finalized by the patron only when promoted, and Spider explicitly does not touch 'new' (per c-mo33ejqy). → [`2026-04-22-design-intake-tier-model-backlog-ready-pool-parked-c-mo1mqd8n.md`](../records/2026-04-22-design-intake-tier-model-backlog-ready-pool-parked-c-mo1mqd8n.md)
20	    - ○ **Design load-bearing blocked_by links as scheduler prerequisites** — Concluded. Design shipped as spider.follows — plugin-owned link meaning, Clerk substrate (type + semanticMeaning split), Spider gate evaluation with conjunctive composition, cycle detection, auto-unstick on recovery, stuck-reason provenance via status.spider slot. → [`2026-04-22-design-load-bearing-blocked_by-links-as-scheduler-c-mo1mqdk0.md`](../records/2026-04-22-design-load-bearing-blocked_by-links-as-scheduler-c-mo1mqdk0.md)
21	
22	**Follow-on threads:**
23	
24	- ○ **Wire Spider to gate dispatch on inbound spider.precedence-gate links — scheduler integration, failed/cancelled-blocker propagation, operator visibility** — Design fully resolved and dispatched as commission w-mo35s0fo-1a1e3cd285bc. Final shape: Spider gates dispatch on inbound spider.follows links (conjunctive composition, polling re-evaluation, cycle detection during gate walk, auto-unstick on recovery, provenance via status.spider slot). → [`2026-04-17-wire-spider-to-gate-dispatch-on-inbound-spiderprec-c-mo2e88aw.md`](../records/2026-04-17-wire-spider-to-gate-dispatch-on-inbound-spiderprec-c-mo2e88aw.md)
25	
26	**Follow-on threads:**
27	
28	- ◇ *(parked)* Visibility gap: 'open' status now overloads several distinct conditions (finalized-and-waiting-for-dependencies, finalized-and-dispatch-eligible, actively-running). Consider whether to introduce sub-states or alternative surfacing (e.g., Oculus column showing gate state) so operators can distinguish at a glance.
29	    - ○ **Design stuck-rig detection and recovery (timeouts + reaper) for the serial hopper** — Goal reframed during investigation — session-level timeout + reaping is already covered by the animator heartbeat reconciler (30s heartbeat / 90s staleness / DLQ on unreachable guild), so the detection half of the original goal was never an actual gap. The real MVP gap was structured stuck-cause observability plus an automated retry primitive, both now shipped via Slices A + B (w-mo81bdcc, w-mo81bdzr) and the field-path fixup (w-mo84lkl7) under the adjacent c-mo28k8ir subtree. → [`2026-04-21-design-stuck-rig-detection-and-recovery-timeouts-r-c-mo28k7ri.md`](../records/2026-04-21-design-stuck-rig-detection-and-recovery-timeouts-r-c-mo28k7ri.md)
30	    - ○ **Design failure and retry policy for autonomous hopper commissions (including retry-starvation fairness)** — Concluded. Retry policy shipped as Slices A + B — w-mo81bdcc (retryable flag + engine-failure stuck cause at every failEngine call site) and w-mo81bdzr (Clockworks-driven stuck→open requeue with N=2 global cap, rigs.length as attempt counter). → [`2026-04-22-design-failure-and-retry-policy-for-autonomous-hop-c-mo28k8ir.md`](../records/2026-04-22-design-failure-and-retry-policy-for-autonomous-hop-c-mo28k8ir.md)
31	
32	**Follow-on threads:**
33	
34	- ● Design patron edit-and-retry — writ brief mutability + requeue vs cancel-and-repost; not a prereq for rig retry primitive (Option 2)
35	    - ● **Design queue observability — surface what's running, queued, stuck, and parked**
36	      - ● Should Oculus surface engine-attempt liveness (process-alive + recent-activity) for running engines, so stuck-vs-running is visible without shelling to ps?
37	    - ● Patron-presence-aware parallelism: Spider's maxConcurrentEngines adjusts based on whether the patron is 'at the console' actively consuming results. When the patron is attending (Coco session active, or an explicit Lattice-delivered 'patron present' signal), run at full parallelism for fast turnaround. When the patron is away, reduce parallelism (or serialize) to avoid wasted work on parallel rigs that the patron will re-review serially anyway. Open questions: presence detection mechanism (active Coco session, Lattice pulse from a dedicated presence-source, idle-timeout inference); the 'away' concurrency ceiling; whether this should interact with Bounty/Levy priorities from the sibling click.
38	    - ● Rig priority metadata (Bounty and Levy): rigs and/or their originating writs carry priority signal that Spider's scheduler honors. Two vocabulary-tome concepts map directly: Bounty = explicit priority tier / reward on a commission (system mapping: commission priority tier affecting dispatch decisions); Levy = patron-originated work takes priority over internally-generated work (system mapping: resource allocation policy ensuring patron work precedes auto-posted petitions). Sean's motivation: directly-dispatched patron commissions should run before auto-detected rigs (e.g. observation-lift child writs, Overseer self-commissions). Combines with patron-presence-aware throttling — when patron is attending, patron-posted (Levy) rigs jump the queue; bounty tiers further order within each class.
39	  - ● Emit a Lattice pulse when an engine exhausts its retry budget (rig terminally fails): the patron should be informed when automatic recovery gives up, not forced to discover the failure by reading dashboards. Proposed trigger type 'reckoner.engine-exhausted' or similar (final name per Reckoner's trigger-namespace conventions). Payload: rig id, writ id, engine id/design, final error, attempt count, history summary. Implementation deferred — this click captures the requirement and links to the engine-retry design (c-mocdm2o7) that produced the need. Delivery substrate is the Lattice as designed in the concluded notifications umbrella (c-mo1z3teo); this is a new trigger type layering on top of that substrate.
40	  - ● Interim mitigation for retry-rig plan-init collision: today's whole-rig auto-retry always spawns a new rig whose plan-init fails with 'Plan "<writId>" already exists' because the original rig's PlanDoc is still there. Today (2026-04-24) 4 writs hit this after a daemon issue caused heartbeat failures across in-flight sessions — the originals went stuck, auto-retry fired, and all 4 retry rigs died at plan-init. The structural fix is c-mocdm2o7 (engine-level retry, no whole-rig recreation), which is already dispatched as commission w-mod0gk8l — but until that lands, every retry attempt poisons the writ with a misleading 'already exists' resolution that masks the original failure cause and requires manual sqlite patching to recover. Question: what's the minimum-effort interim fix? Candidates: (a) disable whole-rig auto-retry entirely until c-mocdm2o7 ships; (b) make plan-init idempotent (adopt-existing if plan id matches); (c) teach Spider to detect 'this writ already has an active rig' and skip retry-spawn; (d) just accept the manual-intervention cost since c-mocdm2o7 is close. Also: when the retry rig fails, the writ's status/resolution gets overwritten with the plan-init error — losing the original heartbeat-failure context; any interim should preserve the original failure signal.
41	  - ○ **Reduce implement-engine per-session cost, which rose ~13× from early to late April without losing the post-Apr-16 quality gains** — Investigation framed as: cost rose 13× from early to late April, mechanism is cache-read compounding driven by task-manifest verify cycles + click-inlined briefs (X011 artifact 2026-04-25-implement-cost-analysis.md has the full analysis). Decomposition (piece-session) considered and ruled out — empirically refuted at our spec scales by X010 H1 (monolithic ran at 0.38× decomposed cost on the same Oculus spec). → [`2026-04-25-reduce-implement-engine-per-session-cost-which-ros-c-modyhqk3.md`](../records/2026-04-25-reduce-implement-engine-per-session-cost-which-ros-c-modyhqk3.md)
42	- ● **Stage 2: Self-correction — detect and address the system's own mistakes without patron prompting**
43	  - ● **Why does the reviewer rarely reject?**
44	    - ● Is the reviewer too weak, or are specs tight enough that pass-through is legitimate?
45	  - ● **Design background monitoring of in-flight commissions (Vigil)**
46	    - ● **How do we detect and recover hung work — anima sessions and engine attempts whose status='running' no longer reflects liveness?**
47	      - ☐ *(task → todo.md)* Detect and recover anima sessions where the LLM is wedged (e.g. internal Bash 'until' loop on a stale PID) — session.status='running' is no longer a liveness signal
48	      - ☐ *(task → todo.md)* Detect and recover orphaned code-engine attempts — engine.status='running' with no liveness check means a dispatcher crash mid-engine leaves the attempt running forever (sibling problem to the anima-session wedge case)
49	      - ● Reviewer wedged on nested 'pnpm -r test' (--test-timeout=0) inside its own babysitter — fresh failure mode (different from internal until-loop); babysitter log reported 0 MCP tool calls proxied over 19 min, suggesting MCP-call-rate-per-minute as a cheap, provider-agnostic liveness signal for the umbrella
50	      - ☐ *(task → todo.md)* Detect and recover anima sessions where the agent ended its turn cleanly (stop_reason=end_turn/stop_sequence, no pending tool_use) but the claude --print process never exits — dangling MCP-proxy SSE connection on 127.0.0.1 keeps node's event loop alive, so babysitter waits forever on close. Observed on rig-moix23cr-7b5e574d (revise engine, ses-moizmsvh-0d927d60): final assistant turn at 19:47Z said 'Work is complete', babysitter and claude child still alive 2h+ later with established TCP pair to MCP proxy port. Heartbeats keep firing so lastActivityAt is misleading — real liveness signal is transcript mtime.
51	      - ☐ *(task → todo.md)* Auto-recover stuck-after-finish sessions via terminal-stop-reason watchdog. Heuristic: when session.status='running' AND last assistant transcript entry has stop_reason in {end_turn, stop_sequence} AND that entry contains no tool_use blocks AND now - transcript-mtime > IDLE_THRESHOLD (e.g. 5–10 min), the agent has finished but the process won't exit. Recovery: SIGTERM the babysitter pgid — its terminal phase will synthesize a result from the accumulated transcript and the rig advances. Could live as a Clockworks standing order or a Spider checker hook. Pairs with the MCP-shutdown bug click as the auto-recovery layer (treat the symptom mechanically while the root-cause investigation continues). Note real liveness signal is transcript mtime, not session.lastActivityAt — heartbeats lie.
52	      - ☐ *(task → todo.md)* Fix the 'babysitter.test.ts' hang — but the root cause turns out to be environmental, not a test bug. Investigation update (2026-04-29 cdd4c6ee): the unit tests themselves are fine. Running 'node --test --test-timeout=30000 src/**/*.test.ts' from a normal shell, all 17 babysitter tests pass in 4 seconds. The '--test-timeout=0' we kept seeing in 'ps' output (along with --inspect-publish-uid=stderr,http --inspect-port=127.0.0.1:9229) is being injected by VS Code's Node.js auto-attach mechanism — the daemon runs in a VS Code-monitored terminal (pts/3 in dev container) and VS Code auto-attach extends the spawned node child's argv with debugger/inspector flags including --test-timeout=0. Anima session babysitters spawned from the daemon inherit the same auto-attach environment, so EVERY 'pnpm test' invocation under any agent session loses its test timeout — mechanical-check steps, animas writing-then-running tests, etc. all fall victim when something legitimately misbehaves. So the 'fix' is closer to environment hygiene than test surgery: (1) make the daemon and its descendants opt out of auto-attach (run with NODE_OPTIONS=--require=... that strips inspector flags, or set 'debug.javascript.autoAttachFilter': 'disabled' in the dev container, or wrap the daemon launch with 'env -u VSCODE_INSPECTOR_OPTIONS' or similar); (2) optionally also set an explicit '--test-timeout=<N>' in package.json test scripts as belt-and-suspenders so even if auto-attach injects 0, the explicit flag takes precedence (last-wins on the node CLI). Originally observed wedging engines on rig-moj12h4o-19af85f4 (review's mechanical-test step) and a reviewer session in 56c811c2; six orphan node --test processes accumulated across sessions and were reaped manually. Related: c-moizriyk (reviewer-wedge symptom), c-moj6u6yi (MCP-shutdown bug, separate symptom), c-moj6ue1g (terminal-stop-reason watchdog, recovery layer).
53	      - ○ **Animator/Spider rate-limit-rejection contract bug. When animate() pre-check rejects a dispatch because the back-off machine is paused (animator.ts ~L635-663), it returns a synthesized rate-limited handle but explicitly does NOT write a SessionDoc — yet the engine has already returned {status:'launched', sessionId} and the rig records that sessionId on its attempt. The Spider's tryCollect later does sessionsBook.get(sessionId), gets null, and the loop's 'if (!session) continue' silently skips the engine forever (spider.ts L1820). The engine sits running on a ghost session id; heartbeats appear to fire (because lastActivityAt updates on read or via residual writes) so the wedge looks like normal in-flight work. Fix: write the rate-limited SessionDoc in the rejection branch — recordSession() with the synthesized rejection result, before returning the handle. Spider's existing rate-limited path (tryCollect L1844) already does the right thing once it can see the doc: handleEngineFailure with rateLimited=true → engine pending + holdReason='animator-paused' → hold clears when window elapses → fresh attempt. Two existing tests assert the no-doc behavior and need to flip: animator.test.ts:1535 (D12, 'pre-check rejection: synthesizes a rate-limited SessionResult when paused') and session-emission.integration.test.ts:304 (rate-limit pre-check rejection path). Observed today on rig-moj1f6m2-3ead937c (review) and rig-moj12h4o-19af85f4 (revise) which both wedged after their dispatch hit the rate-limit window from the implement session of the former rig.** — Fixed directly in efd5b0a (origin/main, shardworks/nexus). animator.ts rejection branch now writes a terminal rate-limited SessionDoc via recordSession() before returning the synthetic handle; the result promise awaits the write so callers (engine collect step on the next crawl tick) see the doc. Two existing tests flipped: animator.test.ts:1535 (was 'no SessionDoc' D12, now 'persists a terminal SessionDoc' c-mojcxzsq); session-emission.integration.test.ts:304 (was 'does NOT emit', now 'emits animator.session.ended exactly once'). → [`2026-04-29-animatorspider-rate-limit-rejection-contract-bug-w-c-mojcxzsq.md`](../records/2026-04-29-animatorspider-rate-limit-rejection-contract-bug-w-c-mojcxzsq.md)
54	      - ● Daemon does not reap zombie babysitter processes. Observed today: babysitter pid 212246 sat as <defunct> (Zs state) under the daemon's pgid for 2h30m+ — the babysitter exited but the daemon never called wait() to collect. Cheap to leave (one process slot each, no other resources) but it's evidence of a missing Node child-process lifecycle hook on the daemon's spawn path. Worth a small audit: trace where the daemon spawns babysitters and confirm there's a 'close' handler that awaits exit (or unrefs/orphans cleanly). Once the rate-limit-rejection fix (c-mojcxzsq, efd5b0a) lands the most common 'engine running while babysitter is dead' shape should clear, but the unreaped-zombie path is structurally independent.
55	      - ☐ *(task → todo.md)* Investigate recurring 'database is locked' errors in daemon stderr. Patterns observed across multiple sessions: '[animator] Failed to update guild_alive_at: database is locked', '[animator] Failed to record transcript for ses-...: database is locked', '[animator] Back-off observer failed for ses-...: database is locked', '[daemon] crawl() error: database is locked'. SQLite WAL mode + busy_timeout should serialize concurrent writers cleanly; persistent 'locked' errors suggest either (a) some write paths bypass the busy-handler retry (raw exec without retry wrapper), (b) a transaction holds a write lock while awaiting an async operation that also needs to write (deadlock-by-async, where the second waiter inside the same JS event loop can never make progress because the first is blocked on its own re-entry), or (c) a long-running read transaction blocking the WAL checkpoint. Investigation steps: enumerate all sessions.put/transcripts.put/state.put call sites and check for missing busy-timeout wrappers; look for any code path where a transaction is opened, then awaits an external promise that also touches the same DB; check WAL file size on disk to see if checkpoints are succeeding. The error doesn't appear fatal — operations are retried implicitly on the next tick — but it represents real work loss (transcript writes the animator gives up on, heartbeats that miss, Spider crawl ticks that abort) and the noise hides genuine failures in the log.
56	  - ● **Close the planner→implementer fidelity gap that let the implementer fabricate razor criteria instead of reading them from the brief**
57	    - ☐ *(task → todo.md)* Make the planner embed canonical content from the brief into the mandate, rather than referencing it by pointer
58	    - ☐ *(task → todo.md)* Make the implementer block (fail loud) when its inputs reference content it cannot access, rather than fabricating plausible substitutes
59	  - ● **Design verification — rig-level automated verification beyond 'tests pass', and guided patron walkthrough with integrated feedback, auto-diagnosis, and fix-commissioning**
60	    - ● What belongs in automated verification within an implement rig beyond 'tests pass' — behavioral assertions, integration scenarios, acceptance-criteria checks, agent-played UAT?

# Unlock autonomous hopper-based operation for Nexus

- **root id**: `c-mo1mq8ry-c8cf4810db6c`
- **status**: live
- **created**: 2026-04-16

---

- ○ **Redesign Astrolabe planner for intent-and-constraints specs with tiered decision model** — Design closed. The planner redesign resolved into: (1) intent-and-constraints specs (not prescriptive implementation) — concluded children c-mo1uw0ah, c-mo1uw15a; (2) binary pick+record / surface model replacing the originally-proposed 4 tiers — concluded child c-mo1mq9qa (tier model scrapped), c-mo1mq9f6 (preference model dropped as not-MVP); (3) a five-criterion razor for which decisions surface — c-mo1yb8aj; (4) three codified policy defaults that account for ~40% of observed overrides — c-mo1yb8nf; (5) empirical baseline of ~3.7% override rate across 38 specs for future comparison — c-mo1yb8zb; (6) ratification-not-approval as the patron-interaction model — c-mo1uw2ml. → [`2026-04-16-redesign-astrolabe-planner-for-intent-and-constrai-c-mo1mq93f.md`](../records/2026-04-16-redesign-astrolabe-planner-for-intent-and-constrai-c-mo1mq93f.md)
- ○ **Choose recovery policy for seal-engine rebase conflicts** — Concluded. Manual-merge recovery tail shipped as commission w-mo2280qu (nexus commit 19921b7) — on Scriptorium seal failure with a rebase-conflict signature, the seal engine grafts a two-engine tail: (1) a manual-merge quick engine summoning the new spider.mender anima to reconcile conflicts in the draft worktree, (2) a retry seal engine attempting fast-forward push. → [`2026-04-22-choose-recovery-policy-for-seal-engine-rebase-conf-c-mo1mqb0r.md`](../records/2026-04-22-choose-recovery-policy-for-seal-engine-rebase-conf-c-mo1mqb0r.md)
- ● **Design concurrency-control substrate for parallel planning rigs**
  - ● Design OCC validation of plans against observed read sets
  - ☐ *(task → todo.md)* Resolve plan-vs-plan conflicts when two Astrolabe sessions overlap
- ○ **Identify what actually predicts patron-override probability, to sharpen the razor beyond shape-matching** — Patron-override rate is not predicted by any single analysis axis (stakes/category/observable/confidence are all weak discriminators — base rate 6.9%, none above 10%). It is predicted by two orthogonal tests: (1) Reach — the choice must radiate beyond the unit being built; pattern-extending decisions do not need patron review, (2) Patch cost — if the outcome is cheap to reverse via follow-up commission, auto-decide. → [`2026-04-17-identify-what-actually-predicts-patron-override-pr-c-mo2c836f.md`](../records/2026-04-17-identify-what-actually-predicts-patron-override-pr-c-mo2c836f.md)
- ● **Stage 1: Run posted commissions unattended — hopper execution and operational guardrails (cost bounds, intervention notifications)**
  - ● Design cost tracking and token budget allocation (Coinmaster/Purse/Tithe)
  - ○ **Implement notifications when the system requires human intervention** — Design fully resolved. Subclicks: triggers (c-mob1vyb2), channel (c-mob1vyyi), response surface (c-mob1w04q), rate-limit (c-mob1w0ql), authoritative source (c-mob1w13b), revised pulse shape (c-mobzl35x supersedes c-mob1vzjy). → [`2026-04-23-implement-notifications-when-the-system-requires-h-c-mo1z3teo.md`](../records/2026-04-23-implement-notifications-when-the-system-requires-h-c-mo1z3teo.md)
  - ● **Deliver autonomous-but-serial hopper operation (maxConcurrentEngines: 1) as the MVP autonomy milestone**
    - ○ **Design intake tier model (backlog / ready pool / parked) to decouple dump from dispatch** — Dissolved. The new/open writ status split already provides the captured-vs-committed decoupling this click was trying to build: writs in 'new' are finalized by the patron only when promoted, and Spider explicitly does not touch 'new' (per c-mo33ejqy). → [`2026-04-22-design-intake-tier-model-backlog-ready-pool-parked-c-mo1mqd8n.md`](../records/2026-04-22-design-intake-tier-model-backlog-ready-pool-parked-c-mo1mqd8n.md)
    - ○ **Design load-bearing blocked_by links as scheduler prerequisites** — Concluded. Design shipped as spider.follows — plugin-owned link meaning, Clerk substrate (type + semanticMeaning split), Spider gate evaluation with conjunctive composition, cycle detection, auto-unstick on recovery, stuck-reason provenance via status.spider slot. → [`2026-04-22-design-load-bearing-blocked_by-links-as-scheduler-c-mo1mqdk0.md`](../records/2026-04-22-design-load-bearing-blocked_by-links-as-scheduler-c-mo1mqdk0.md)

**Follow-on threads:**

- ○ **Wire Spider to gate dispatch on inbound spider.precedence-gate links — scheduler integration, failed/cancelled-blocker propagation, operator visibility** — Design fully resolved and dispatched as commission w-mo35s0fo-1a1e3cd285bc. Final shape: Spider gates dispatch on inbound spider.follows links (conjunctive composition, polling re-evaluation, cycle detection during gate walk, auto-unstick on recovery, provenance via status.spider slot). → [`2026-04-17-wire-spider-to-gate-dispatch-on-inbound-spiderprec-c-mo2e88aw.md`](../records/2026-04-17-wire-spider-to-gate-dispatch-on-inbound-spiderprec-c-mo2e88aw.md)

**Follow-on threads:**

- ◇ *(parked)* Visibility gap: 'open' status now overloads several distinct conditions (finalized-and-waiting-for-dependencies, finalized-and-dispatch-eligible, actively-running). Consider whether to introduce sub-states or alternative surfacing (e.g., Oculus column showing gate state) so operators can distinguish at a glance.
    - ○ **Design stuck-rig detection and recovery (timeouts + reaper) for the serial hopper** — Goal reframed during investigation — session-level timeout + reaping is already covered by the animator heartbeat reconciler (30s heartbeat / 90s staleness / DLQ on unreachable guild), so the detection half of the original goal was never an actual gap. The real MVP gap was structured stuck-cause observability plus an automated retry primitive, both now shipped via Slices A + B (w-mo81bdcc, w-mo81bdzr) and the field-path fixup (w-mo84lkl7) under the adjacent c-mo28k8ir subtree. → [`2026-04-21-design-stuck-rig-detection-and-recovery-timeouts-r-c-mo28k7ri.md`](../records/2026-04-21-design-stuck-rig-detection-and-recovery-timeouts-r-c-mo28k7ri.md)
    - ○ **Design failure and retry policy for autonomous hopper commissions (including retry-starvation fairness)** — Concluded. Retry policy shipped as Slices A + B — w-mo81bdcc (retryable flag + engine-failure stuck cause at every failEngine call site) and w-mo81bdzr (Clockworks-driven stuck→open requeue with N=2 global cap, rigs.length as attempt counter). → [`2026-04-22-design-failure-and-retry-policy-for-autonomous-hop-c-mo28k8ir.md`](../records/2026-04-22-design-failure-and-retry-policy-for-autonomous-hop-c-mo28k8ir.md)

**Follow-on threads:**

- ● Design patron edit-and-retry — writ brief mutability + requeue vs cancel-and-repost; not a prereq for rig retry primitive (Option 2)
    - ● **Design queue observability — surface what's running, queued, stuck, and parked**
      - ● Should Oculus surface engine-attempt liveness (process-alive + recent-activity) for running engines, so stuck-vs-running is visible without shelling to ps?
    - ● Patron-presence-aware parallelism: Spider's maxConcurrentEngines adjusts based on whether the patron is 'at the console' actively consuming results. When the patron is attending (Coco session active, or an explicit Lattice-delivered 'patron present' signal), run at full parallelism for fast turnaround. When the patron is away, reduce parallelism (or serialize) to avoid wasted work on parallel rigs that the patron will re-review serially anyway. Open questions: presence detection mechanism (active Coco session, Lattice pulse from a dedicated presence-source, idle-timeout inference); the 'away' concurrency ceiling; whether this should interact with Bounty/Levy priorities from the sibling click.
    - ● Rig priority metadata (Bounty and Levy): rigs and/or their originating writs carry priority signal that Spider's scheduler honors. Two vocabulary-tome concepts map directly: Bounty = explicit priority tier / reward on a commission (system mapping: commission priority tier affecting dispatch decisions); Levy = patron-originated work takes priority over internally-generated work (system mapping: resource allocation policy ensuring patron work precedes auto-posted petitions). Sean's motivation: directly-dispatched patron commissions should run before auto-detected rigs (e.g. observation-lift child writs, Overseer self-commissions). Combines with patron-presence-aware throttling — when patron is attending, patron-posted (Levy) rigs jump the queue; bounty tiers further order within each class.
  - ● Emit a Lattice pulse when an engine exhausts its retry budget (rig terminally fails): the patron should be informed when automatic recovery gives up, not forced to discover the failure by reading dashboards. Proposed trigger type 'reckoner.engine-exhausted' or similar (final name per Reckoner's trigger-namespace conventions). Payload: rig id, writ id, engine id/design, final error, attempt count, history summary. Implementation deferred — this click captures the requirement and links to the engine-retry design (c-mocdm2o7) that produced the need. 1	# Todo

Out-of-band cleanup — no new commission; a Stacks-layer declarative index-drop mechanism is the proper long-term fix when another commission warrants it.** (`c-mo39vak3`)

27

Whatever handler design wins (a/b/c/d), it produces provenance-tagged supplements (patron-fabricated answers, bold-agent decisions, expert-panel recommendations, etc.) that must integrate into the Sage's working contract alongside the distillate. Open questions: do these supplements live as separate document sections or merge into the distillate? How does Sage weight them vs. the distillate's directly-distilled content? 44	
45	- [ ] **Resolve the seal-engine workspace conflict — session container vs bare clone on host** (`c-mo3ibfq3`)
46	- [ ] **Move rig construction from Spider to the Fabricator** (`c-mo3ibgzt`)
47	
48	## Unlock autonomous hopper-based operation for Nexus
49	
50	- [ ] **Resolve plan-vs-plan conflicts when two Astrolabe sessions overlap** (`c-mo1mqcwz`)
51	- [ ] **Detect and recover anima sessions where the LLM is wedged (e.g. internal Bash 'until' loop on a stale PID) — session.status='running' is no longer a liveness signal** (`c-moevx2i0`)
52	- [ ] **Detect and recover orphaned code-engine attempts — engine.status='running' with no liveness check means a dispatcher crash mid-engine leaves the attempt running forever (sibling problem to the anima-session wedge case)** (`c-moildpau`)
53	- [ ] **Detect and recover anima sessions where the agent ended its turn cleanly (stop_reason=end_turn/stop_sequence, no pending tool_use) but the claude --print process never exits — dangling MCP-proxy SSE connection on 127.0.0.1 keeps node's event loop alive, so babysitter waits forever on close. Observed on rig-moix23cr-7b5e574d (revise engine, ses-moizmsvh-0d927d60): final assistant turn at 19:47Z said 'Work is complete', babysitter and claude child still alive 2h+ later with established TCP pair to MCP proxy port. Heartbeats keep firing so lastActivityAt is misleading — real liveness signal is transcript mtime.** (`c-moj6u6yi`)
54	- [ ] **Auto-recover stuck-after-finish sessions via terminal-stop-reason watchdog. Heuristic: when session.status='running' AND last assistant transcript entry has stop_reason in {end_turn, stop_sequence} AND that entry contains no tool_use blocks AND now - transcript-mtime > IDLE_THRESHOLD (e.g. 5–10 min), the agent has finished but the process won't exit. Recovery: SIGTERM the babysitter pgid — its terminal phase will synthesize a result from the accumulated transcript and the rig advances. Could live as a Clockworks standing order or a Spider checker hook. Pairs with the MCP-shutdown bug click as the auto-recovery layer (treat the symptom mechanically while the root-cause investigation continues). Note real liveness signal is transcript mtime, not session.lastActivityAt — heartbeats lie.** (`c-moj6ue1g`)
55	- [ ] **Fix the 'babysitter.test.ts' hang — but the root cause turns out to be environmental, not a test bug. Investigation update (2026-04-29 cdd4c6ee): the unit tests themselves are fine. Running 'node --test --test-timeout=30000 src/**/*.test.ts' from a normal shell, all 17 babysitter tests pass in 4 seconds. The '--test-timeout=0' we kept seeing in 'ps' output (along with --inspect-publish-uid=stderr,http --inspect-port=127.0.0.1:9229) is being injected by VS Code's Node.js auto-attach mechanism — the daemon runs in a VS Code-monitored terminal (pts/3 in dev container) and VS Code auto-attach extends the spawned node child's argv with debugger/inspector flags including --test-timeout=0. Anima session babysitters spawned from the daemon inherit the same auto-attach environment, so EVERY 'pnpm test' invocation under any agent session loses its test timeout — mechanical-check steps, animas writing-then-running tests, etc. all fall victim when something legitimately misbehaves. So the 'fix' is closer to environment hygiene than test surgery: (1) make the daemon and its descendants opt out of auto-attach (run with NODE_OPTIONS=--require=... that strips inspector flags, or set 'debug.javascript.autoAttachFilter': 'disabled' in the dev container, or wrap the daemon launch with 'env -u VSCODE_INSPECTOR_OPTIONS' or similar); (2) optionally also set an explicit '--test-timeout=<N>' in package.json test scripts as belt-and-suspenders so even if auto-attach injects 0, the explicit flag takes precedence (last-wins on the node CLI). Originally observed wedging engines on rig-moj12h4o-19af85f4 (review's mechanical-test step) and a reviewer session in 56c811c2; six orphan node --test processes accumulated across sessions and were reaped manually. Related: c-moizriyk (reviewer-wedge symptom), c-moj6u6yi (MCP-shutdown bug, separate symptom), c-moj6ue1g (terminal-stop-reason watchdog, recovery layer).** (`c-moj7h6r7`)
56	- [ ] **Investigate recurring 'database is locked' errors in daemon stderr. Patterns observed across multiple sessions: '[animator] Failed to update guild_alive_at: database is locked', '[animator] Failed to record transcript for ses-...: database is locked', '[animator] Back-off observer failed for ses-...: database is locked', '[daemon] crawl() error: database is locked'. SQLite WAL mode + busy_timeout should serialize concurrent writers cleanly; persistent 'locked' errors suggest either (a) some write paths bypass the busy-handler retry (raw exec without retry wrapper), (b) a transaction holds a write lock while awaiting an async operation that also needs to write (deadlock-by-async, where the second waiter inside the same JS event loop can never make progress because the first is blocked on its own re-entry), or (c) a long-running read transaction blocking the WAL checkpoint. Investigation steps: enumerate all sessions.put/transcripts.put/state.put call sites and check for missing busy-timeout wrappers; look for any code path where a transaction is opened, then awaits an external promise that also touches the same DB; check WAL file size on disk to see if checkpoints are succeeding. The error doesn't appear fatal — operations are retried implicitly on the next tick — but it represents real work loss (transcript writes the animator gives up on, heartbeats that miss, Spider crawl ticks that abort) and the noise hides genuine failures in the log.** (`c-mojdtg62`)
57	- [ ] **Make the planner embed canonical content from the brief into the mandate, rather than referencing it by pointer** (`c-mo21apns`)
58	- [ ] **Make the implementer block (fail loud) when its inputs reference content it cannot access, rather than fabricating plausible substitutes** (`c-mo21aqo6`)
59	- [ ] **Extract testable deliverables from brief/spec — produce a structured walkthrough list of what the patron should be able to do after the commission lands** (`c-mo3dj7v7`)
60	- [ ] **Ship MVP Overseer: nsg overseer commission — promote one draft writ from the new pool to open** (`c-moaz1q9b`)
61	- [ ] **Add named Clockworks events for petition transitions — emit petition.accepted, petition.deferred, petition.declined, petition.stalled (and possibly petition.fresh) as named Clockworks events alongside the existing auto-wired book.reckoner.reckonings.created CDC channel. Rejected in the Reckonings book design (Open Question 5) on grounds that auto-wired CDC + in-relay outcome filter serves every Clockworks consumer; revisit when (a) a non-Clockworks consumer of the petition stream appears (external webhook, dashboard service) that benefits from a flat named-event payload, (b) measurable performance penalty appears in the in-relay filter path, or (c) the Clockworks signal validator's reserved-namespace policy gains teeth on book.* events and the same pass extends teeth to petition.*. Implementation note: the named-events emitter would naturally hang off the same CDC handler as the staleness snapshot updater (sibling click c-moixpj1l) — both are derived signals over the Reckonings stream, so colocating keeps the cross-cutting concerns in one place.** (`c-moj6mjep`)

content.text:
Done. The export is on disk at `/workspace/nexus-mk2/threads/`, `records/`, and `todo.md`. Stats and observations:

## What's there

- **47 active thread files** in `threads/` (one per top-level live/parked click subtree)
- **4 archived thread files** in `threads/_archive/` (top-level concluded clicks that have substantive subtrees)
- **312 records** in `records/` (every concluded + dropped click)
- **24 tasks** in `todo.md` (live leaf clicks with imperative-verb shape)

Disk: threads/ is 388K total, records/ is 1.3M, todo is 11K.

Thread file size distribution is bimodal: 4 huge ones (22K–72K, the major umbrellas: hopper-autonomy, implement-cost, planning-quality, laboratory) and ~40 modest ones (most under 5K).

## What to look at first

A spread of files that show different shapes:

- `threads/how-should-cocos-click-based-session-continuity-wo-c-mo1itggx.md` — the meta-thread itself; clean small example
- `threads/build-the-laboratory-apparatus-...md` — medium-sized active build, mix of decisions + open work
- `threads/unlock-autonomous-hopper-based-operation-for-nexus-c-mo1mq8ry.md` — the **hardest case**: the biggest umbrella, deep nesting, the real stress test
- `records/2026-04-28-design-the-reckoner-scheduler-kit-contribution-sha-c-moisx6fx.md` — sample heavy decision record (full markdown rendering of a substantive technical decision)
- `records/2026-04-23-design-the-canonical-vocabulary-alias-registry-mac-c-mobzw7uc.md` — sample lightweight record
- `todo.md` — the whole thing

## What works well in this form (Coco-side honest read)

- **Thread files are genuinely scannable.** I can see the shape of the laboratory build at a glance — concluded design decisions inline-summarized, open implementation work, parked v2 items, separated visually by status marker.
- **Records carry the full weight without polluting the thread.** The 5K Reckoner-scheduler record has tables, code blocks, headers — things conclusions never accommodated. Promoted to first-class artifact, the long-form actually reads like a decision record should.
- **Todo is a flat list you can work down.** No tree navigation needed.
- **Filesystem-native.** Open in editor, grep, git diff, github browse — all just work.

## What's awkward (worth seeing for yourself)

1. **Inline decision summaries are still long.** The summaries in thread files come from the existing concluded-click conclusions, which are 1,000+ char essays under the old discipline. In a fresh system, summaries would be shorter by design. So the threads look more verbose than they would natively. Read past this; it's a legacy artifact, not a system flaw.

2. **The biggest thread (hopper-autonomy, 72K) is too much.** That's a real signal — some umbrellas are too big for a single file. Either we'd want sub-thread files (per-stage in this case: stage1.md, stage2.md, stage3.md) or a different decomposition strategy. The current export tried to keep one umbrella = one file; that doesn't scale to truly large arcs.

3. **Todo grouping is ugly.** The `## ` headers are truncated thread titles ("## Address fallout from the engine-level retry refactor (ace0ee"). Real version would link to thread files or use clean topic names. Cosmetic but jarring.

4. **Task classification is approximate.** Some "tasks" in todo.md are really investigations or open questions ("Audit the /testing subpath surface…", "Investigate recurring 'database is locked' errors"). The heuristic worked OK but ~30% feel borderline. A real adoption would probably want explicit type-at-creation rather than post-hoc classification.

5. **Some tasks have paragraph-length descriptions.** The babysitter-hang and database-locked entries in todo.md are huge — those started life as bloated click goals because there was nowhere to put context. In a fresh system, a "task" would point to a thread or record for context and the task entry would be 1–2 lines.

6. **"Follow-on threads" sub-section duplication.** Look at the Laboratory thread around line 17 — when a parent click is concluded but has live children, my renderer dumps a "Follow-on threads" header for each. That's a rendering bug, not an architecture problem, but it's visible.

7. **47 thread files is a lot to see in `ls`.** You'd want some grouping/ordering — recently-touched first, or grouped by area (laboratory/, planning/, infrastructure/, meta/). Flat directory at this volume is workable but not great.

## Honest comparison

Reading through the export, I keep noticing one thing: **for active work, the thread files are doing what `nsg click extract` was supposed to do, but better** — they're already markdown-shaped (LLM-friendly), they're browsable in your editor (you-friendly), they preserve hierarchy (decomposition-friendly), and they have stable filenames you can reference.

For the long tail of concluded decisions, **the records dir is the durable historical ledger** — 312 individual files is a lot, but each is small and they're genuinely searchable. `grep -r` over `records/` would beat `nsg click list --status concluded` for finding "what did we decide about X."

For the live pile, **todo.md collapses 24 tasks into one short list** — vs. `nsg click tree` showing them buried under various umbrellas with full context.

The thing this *loses* vs. clicks is the live SQLite-backed query layer: I can't say `nsg click list --has-no-conclusion-and-touched-since-7d` against the export. But I'm not sure how often I actually use that. Most of my real queries are "what's the shape of this topic" (now: cat the thread file) and "what was decided about X" (now: grep records/).

Want to spend a few minutes browsing the files in your editor? I'd particularly value your reaction to the hopper-autonomy thread (the worst case) and a few records (to see if the long-form-as-artifact feels right). Then we can decide.
